junior.question: Why is dynamic padding during collating more memory-efficient than padding all sequences to a fixed context length?
junior.core_intent: Checks optimization reasoning in dataset loader processing.
junior.preparation_guide: Explain pad token reductions and sequence-level batch scaling.
junior.explanation: Dynamic padding aligns sequences in each batch to the maximum length found *within that specific batch* rather than the maximum context length (e.g. 2048). This minimizes redundant token processing and saves GPU memory.
junior.star_framework_tip: Highlight sequence length's quadratic effect on self-attention memory usage.
common_pitfalls: Believing that dynamic padding improves model accuracy (it only improves computational throughput).

junior.question: Why is deduplicating training data important for language-model quality?
junior.explanation: Duplicates make the model over-memorize repeated text, waste compute, and can leak test content into training; removing them improves generalization and data efficiency, which matters most for small-capacity models.
junior.star_framework_tip: State the three harms duplicates cause.

references.title: Deduplicating Training Data Makes LMs Better

junior.question: What does perplexity measure, in plain terms?
junior.explanation: Perplexity is the exponential of the average per-token cross-entropy on held-out text; intuitively it is how surprised the model is by real text, with lower meaning better prediction. It is comparable only across the same data and tokenizer.
junior.star_framework_tip: Give the intuition plus the same-tokenizer caveat.

junior.question: Why keep a validation set separate from the test set?
junior.explanation: Validation guides tuning and checkpoint selection, while the test set is touched once for an unbiased final estimate; tuning on the test set leaks information and overstates generalization.
junior.star_framework_tip: Separate 'tune on validation' from 'measure once on test.'

mid_level.question: What is the difference between causal language model training loss (labels shifted by one) and standard multi-class classification loss?
mid_level.core_intent: Tests structural knowledge of next-token predictions and cross entropy targets.
mid_level.preparation_guide: Describe shifted logits mapping to labels for autoregressive predictions.
mid_level.explanation: In causal language modeling, the inputs are matched against labels shifted by one token to the right. The objective is to predict token $t_{i+1}$ given sequence $t_{1..i}$. In standard classification, a single label is predicted for the entire sequence.
mid_level.star_framework_tip: Write out shifting indices math mappings ($logits[..., :-1, :]$ vs $labels[..., 1:]$).
common_pitfalls: Not shifting logits when calculating cross-entropy loss in manual loops, leading to high training error.

mid_level.question: Why does the effective batch size (with gradient accumulation) matter when tuning the learning rate?
mid_level.preparation_guide: Connect effective batch to gradient noise and LR.
mid_level.explanation: Gradient accumulation makes the effective batch larger than the physical batch, which reduces gradient noise and typically supports (and may require) a higher, well-warmed learning rate. Tuning the learning rate against the physical batch alone misjudges the optimization dynamics, since the optimizer steps on the accumulated effective batch.
mid_level.star_framework_tip: Tie effective batch to gradient noise and the appropriate learning rate.
common_pitfalls: Setting the learning rate from the physical batch and ignoring accumulation.

mid_level.question: To choose between two summarization checkpoints, how would you combine perplexity and ROUGE, and what are their limits?
mid_level.preparation_guide: Explain using both plus a semantic/human check.
mid_level.explanation: Perplexity indicates general modeling quality but not summarization usefulness, while ROUGE measures content overlap with reference summaries but misses paraphrase and faithfulness. Use ROUGE as the task signal, perplexity as a sanity check, and add a semantic metric (BERTScore) or human/LLM-judged faithfulness review before deciding, since neither alone captures factual correctness.
mid_level.star_framework_tip: Combine ROUGE (task) + perplexity (sanity) + a semantic/faithfulness check.
common_pitfalls: Picking the checkpoint with lower perplexity regardless of summary quality.

references.title: ROUGE: A Package for Automatic Evaluation of Summaries (Lin)
BERTScore: Evaluating Text Generation with BERT

mid_level.question: How does data contamination inflate evaluation scores, and how do you detect it?
mid_level.preparation_guide: Explain memorized test data and overlap detection.
mid_level.explanation: If evaluation examples (or near-duplicates) appear in training data, the model can recall rather than generalize, inflating scores and masking real weaknesses. Detect it by scanning training data for n-gram or hash overlap with eval sets, checking suspiciously high scores on memorization-prone items, and comparing against freshly created held-out examples.
mid_level.star_framework_tip: Link memorized eval data to inflated scores and name overlap detection.
common_pitfalls: Trusting high benchmark scores without a contamination check.

references.title: Deduplicating Training Data Makes LMs Better

senior.question: Explain how gradient norm clipping stabilizes training runs, and contrast clipping gradient norms with clipping gradient values.
senior.core_intent: Tests numerical optimization and vector scaling understanding.
senior.preparation_guide: Explain vector scale division vs individual index truncation.
senior.explanation: Clipping gradient norms calculates the global norm of all parameters combined ($G_{norm}$) and scales gradients by $max\_norm / G_{norm}$ if they exceed the threshold. This preserves the gradient vector's direction. Clipping gradient values truncates individual gradient coordinates independently, which alters the direction of parameter updates and can hinder optimization.
senior.star_framework_tip: Demonstrate the mathematical scale invariance of the gradient direction under norm clipping.
common_pitfalls: Confusing clipping gradients with weight decay configurations.

senior.question: Design an end-to-end path from raw corpus to a trained, evaluated checkpoint with leakage controls.
senior.preparation_guide: Sequence the stages with decontamination before training and rigorous eval after.
senior.explanation: Curate and clean the corpus, deduplicate, and decontaminate against all eval sets before tokenizing and packing. Train with a sensible schedule, mixed precision, checkpointing, and best-model selection on validation. Evaluate the final checkpoint with task metrics plus perplexity, multiple seeds and confidence intervals, and a final contamination re-check, retaining per-example outputs. Each stage gates the next.
senior.star_framework_tip: Present clean -> dedup -> decontaminate -> train -> rigorous eval as gated stages.
common_pitfalls: Running evaluation without a decontamination gate, invalidating the numbers.

references.title: Deduplicating Training Data Makes LMs Better

senior.question: Explain why low perplexity does not guarantee good downstream or task performance.
senior.preparation_guide: Explain the mismatch between modeling and tasks.
senior.explanation: Perplexity rewards predicting the next token of generic text, which does not directly measure reasoning, instruction following, factual accuracy, or formatting. A model can have low perplexity yet fail a task because the task needs capabilities the metric does not probe, or because evaluation requires a specific output format. Always confirm with task-specific, contamination-checked evaluation.
senior.star_framework_tip: Contrast token prediction with task capabilities and require task eval.
common_pitfalls: Shipping a model on perplexity alone without task validation.
